content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Bill Sartor 
City Attorney 
City of Tracy 
333 Civic Center Plaza 
Tracy, CA 95376 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-17-096 
Dear Mr. Sartor: 
June 13, 2017 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Tracy Mayor Pro Tern Veronica 
Vargas regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Refonn Act (the "Act").1 Please 
note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not 
under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or 
Section I 090.:? We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby ( 1975) 1 FPPC 
Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. 
QUESTIONS 
I. Whether Trumark Home's ("Trumark") on-going negotiations with Surland Communities 
LLC ("Surland"), to acquire real property within the Ellis Project ("Project"), prevent Mayor Pro 
Tern Vargas from participating in decisions regarding the Project, including land use approvals and 
professional services agreements for various consultants to conduct work in processing those 
applications? 
2. If Trumark acquires real property within the Project, does the Act prevent Mayor Pro 
Tern Vargas from participating in decisions for other development applications, such as Phase II, 
within the Project? Or, if properly segmented pursuant to Regulation 18706, can Mayor Pro Tern 
Vargas participate in decisions for other development applications within the Project in the event 
Trumark acquires property within the Project? 
1 The Pohttcal Refonn Acl is contained in Go\ emment Code Sec1ions 81000 1hrough 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Go\'emment Code. unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Poliucal Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2. Division 6 of the Cahfomia Code of Regulations. unless otherwise indicated. 
? On June 6. 2017, your office mfonncd us that Mayor Pro Tern Vargas is no longer employed by Trumark. 
Therefore. we have not included a Section I 090 analysis. 


File No. A-17-096 
Page No. 2 
3. lfTrumark acquires property within the Project, may Mayor Pro Tern Vargas participate 
in decisions for development projects adjacent to the Project that impact Surland's obligations 
under the Ellis Project Development Agreement? 
4. May Mayor Pro Tern Vargas participate in decisions regarding amenities within the 
Project, including Council actions regarding the design or funding of the proposed swim center: (a) 
during Trumark's on-going negotiations with Surland to acquire property within the Project and (b) 
if Trumark Homes acquires property within the Project? 
CONCLUSIONS 
1-4. Because these decisions would have a financial effect on Trumark, the Act would 
preclude Mayor Pro Tern Vargas from any involvement in these decisions.3 
FACTS 
Mayor Pro Tem Vargas 
Mayor Pro Tern Vargas has served on the Tracy City Council since December 2014, and 
had been employed by Trumark as its Director of Development since April 2013. On June 6, 
2017, your office informed us that Mayor Pro Tern Vargas is no longer employed by Trumark. 
Her duties with Trumark involved project management, due diligence, entitlements, permitting, 
and research regarding jurisdictional requirements, project conditions, and general plan and 
zoning requirements. 
You state that Trumark has not had any interest in projects within the City of Tracy; that 
Mayor Pro Tern Vargas' duties have not involved any projects within the City; and that Trumark 
Homes does not provide project-specific bonuses or special compensation to staff based on the 
successful completion of projects. 
Mayor Pro Tern Vargas has received over $2,000 in income from Trumark in the last twelve 
months. In a telephone conversation on May 24, 2017, you noted that Trumark has approximately 
100 employees and that Mayor Pro Tem Vargas has no ownership interest in Trumark. 
11,c Project 
In April 2013, the City approved an amended and restated Development Abrreement, 
Amended Specific Plan, and Financing and Implementation Plan ("FIP") with Surland which 
vested the development rights. for the approximately 321-acres known as the Project.4 Surland is the 
owner/developer of a large residential development project. The Project includes residential and 
1 While under certain circumstances a public official disqualified from one decision may participate in other 
related decisions provided that the official"s participation does not affect the dec1s1on in which he or she has a conflict 
of interest. without more informauon regarding a specific decision. we are unable to advise as to whether segmentation 
would allow Mayor Pro Tern Vargas to participate in a particular decision. 
4 The City had previously negotiated and appro\·ed a Development Agreement and \arious project approvals 
for the Ellis Project between 2006 and 1008, however. due to litigation, the Development Agreement and those 
approvals had to be re-processed. 


File No. A-17-096 
Page No. 3 
retail/commercial development and a proposed swim center. Once completed, the Project will 
consist of approximately 2,250 residential units and approximately 35 acres of retail/commercial, 
and other non-residential space. 
The Project is one of the largest developments in the City and, given its size, the 
development of this site is being completed in phases. The City has hired various consultants to 
assist with the processing of development applications for the Project such as engineers to conduct 
analyses for the construction of public improvements; the costs associated with these consultants 
are recouped from Surland or the applicable developer through a cost recovery agreement. 
The Project is subdivided into large parcels, each with its own tentative subdivision map, 
which are in tum, typically acquired by other developers who build homes and present the final map 
for Council approval. The approval of a final map is ministerial; however, a final map is often 
accompanied by discretionary approvals such as contracts regarding improvements (e.g. 
Subdivision Improvement A!,rreements, Offsite Improvement Agreements, and/or Deferred 
Improvement Abrreements) which require City Council approval. Phase IA of the Project is 
currently being completed by a different developer (or subdivider) which acquired the subdivision 
from Surland. Council approved the final map and a subdivision improvement agreement for Phase 
IA on September t, 2015. 
Surland recently applied to amend the Ellis Specific Plan and a request to amend the 
existing Development A1:,ireement, both of which require City Council approval. If approved, these 
amendments may change the zoning for the property, and financial and other obligations for the 
Project relating to fees and land dedication requirements including those relating to the proposed 
swim center.5 The City is also currently processing a development application submitted by 
Surland for Phase II of the Ellis Project which includes a tentative subdivision map consisting of 
approximately 356 residential lots. Combined, these applications seek to amend key facets of the 
original vested rights approvals, which may impact the marketability of the Project as whole, or a 
specific phase of the Project. 
The properties adjacent to the Project are also the subject of various pending development 
applications the City is cutTcntly reviewing. Those applications, while not specifically about the 
Project property, may impact the Project because of the properties' shared infrastructure and 
Surland's obligations regarding that infrastructure. The pending applications require consideration 
of the location of storm drain detention basins, determining the responsible party for the 
construction of certain improvements, assignment of fees to pay for the construction of the 
improvements, and adoption of a Specific Plan. These applications require Council approval. 
In July 201 Ɠ. Mayor Pro Tem Vargas infonned your office that Tnnnark and Surland were 
negotiating the potential purchase of residential lots within the Project. Since then. you state that 
Mayor Pro Tern Vargas had recused herself from all Council actions pertaining to the Project. 
According to Mayor Pro Tern Vargas. Trumark had limited her role in the potential acquisition and 
was not part of the Trumark team working on the potential purchase oflots from Surland. 
± The Development Agreement currently requires that the Dc\'elopcr dedicate land for and contribute 
SI 0.000,000 to fund the design, construction. operation and maintenance of a swim center. 


ANALYSIS 
File No. A-17-096 
Page No. 4 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. 
(Section 87103.) Section 82048 defines "public official" as every member, officer, employee, or 
consultant of a state or local government agency, including city councilmembers. Interests from 
which a conflict of interest may arise are defined in Section 87103 and include the following: 
• Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in the 
regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, 
aggregating $500 or more in value provided to, received by, or promised to, the public 
official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. 
According to your facts, Mayor Pro Tern Vargas has an interest in Trumark as a source of 
income within the preceding 12 months. (Section 87103(c).) 
Foreseeability 
A financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable when it is explicitly involved in 
a decision. Financial interests that are explicitly involved include an interest that is a named party 
in, or subject of, a government decision. A financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the 
decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or 
other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest. (Regulation 1870 l (a).) 
Matcrialitv: Business £11/itv as Source of b1comc 
. 
. 
. 
Regulation 18702.1 provides the standard for materiality when the income is for services or 
a salary and the source is a business entity. (Regulation 18702.3(a)(4).) Under Regulation 
18702.1 (a), as relevant to these facts, the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a decision is 
material if the business entity: 
(3) Bids on or enters into a written contract with the official's agency; or 
* * * 
(5) Applies for a permit, license, grant, tax credit, exception. variance, or other 
entitlement that the official's agency is authorized to issue 
. 
' 
The decisions at issue involve land use approvals and professional services agreements with 
Surland, which has vested development rights for the Project, and is currently in negotiations with 
Trumark for the purchase of residential lots within the Project. Based on the facts presented. it is 
reasonably foreseeable that decisions relating to the Ellis Project will have a material financial 
effect on Trumark, which remains a disqualifying source of income to Mayor Pro Tern Vargas for a 
period of 12 months following the last time she was provided, promised, or received, income 
aggregating S500 or more from Trumark. 


File No. A-17-096 
Page No. 5 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
Sincerely, 
Jack Woodside 
Acting General Counsel 
By: '3· rTJJ;. 
Zachary W. Norton 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division 
ZWN:jgl
